# XN-018 — Orchestrator vs prior implementations (full 17-task v2)

*Generated by research-copilot*

> **⚠ STATUS — PRELIMINARY, NOT VALIDATED.** The adversarial code review
> (RN-005, fresh-context Claude subagent dispatched after this doc was
> drafted) identified **3 P1 blockers** that materially affect the +0.204
> headline delta. Three of the largest-win tasks (TutorialSneddon,
> ExampleIsothermalLeakyWell, ExampleDPWellbore) are implicated by at
> least one of: cross-test-task GT leakage, unenforced `Write` tool ban,
> or near-GT bootstrap copy. Token totals are inflated 2–4× by JSONL
> double-counting. The numbers below are reproduced as-of-claim; do not
> propagate without the fixes in §Response. See RN-005 for full evidence.

## Question

Does the 5-subagent orchestrator (D-010 / XN-017) outperform the prior
single-agent implementations on the full 17-task v2 eval set when given
the same backbone model (DSv4-flash)? On three axes: quality (TreeSim),
efficiency (wall-clock at fixed concurrency), and token usage.

## Headline

**Yes.** With DSv4-flash as the backbone:

- **Quality.** Orchestrator mean TreeSim **0.851** (median 0.852, n=17, all
  succeeded). Same-model paired delta vs vanilla DSv4-flash: **+0.204**
  mean, 13 wins / 3 losses / 1 tie. The orchestrator essentially closes the
  gap between DSv4-flash and OpenHands + minimax-m2.7 (0.863) — a
  substantially larger backbone — while using a smaller, cheaper model.
- **Efficiency.** ≈2.2× more compute than vanilla DSv4-flash (15055s vs
  6825s sum). True wall-clock at the campaign's actual concurrency: 134
  min @ W=2 vs 21 min @ W=6. Orchestrator wall is dominated by serial
  subagent fan-out (5 sequential subagent invocations per task), and the
  campaign was deliberately conservative on `--workers` (2) to limit
  docker-in-docker pressure.
- **Tokens.** *Lower* paid input tokens than vanilla (**4.4M vs 6.9M**),
  *higher* cache-read tokens (**128M vs 72M**). Each subagent sees a
  smaller, focused primer; the same primer is reused across 5 subagents
  per task, hitting the prompt cache hard. Token-per-quality is the best
  among all DSv4-flash variants.

## Setup

- **Eval set**: 17 v2 tasks (`misc/memory_artifacts/test_blocklist.json`
  keys).
- **Backbone**: DSv4-flash via `https://api.deepseek.com/anthropic`.
- **Metric**: TreeSim (`overall_01` from `<task>_eval.json`, 0–1).
- **Per-task wall clock**: `elapsed_seconds` from `status.json`.
- **Campaign wall**: `max(ended) − min(started)` across all task
  status.json files. For the orchestrator runner (which doesn't record
  start/end timestamps in status.json), falls back to filesystem mtimes
  on `eval_metadata.json` and `status.json`.
- **Tokens**: summed `message.usage.input_tokens` /
  `cache_read_input_tokens` from `claude_stdout.json` /
  `acpx_output.json` JSONL. Note: DSv4-flash via the Anthropic-compatible
  endpoint reports `output_tokens=0` for all turns — the field is
  populated by Anthropic's API but not by the DeepSeek shim. So
  "tok in+out" for DSv4-flash variants is effectively input only;
  OpenHands reports prompt and completion explicitly.
- **Orchestrator runs**: union of `orch_dsv4_5task_s1` (cycle 5 from
  XN-017, 5 tasks) and `orch_dsv4_remain12_s1` (this experiment, 12 tasks).
  All 17 tasks completed cleanly; xmllint-valid in all cases.

## Implementations compared

| Agent | Model | Plugin | Workers | Run dir |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **orchestrator-DSv4flash** | DSv4-flash | `plugin_orchestrator/` (5 subagents) | 2 | `orch_dsv4_5task_s1` ∪ `orch_dsv4_remain12_s1` |
| vanilla-DSv4flash | DSv4-flash | none, no primer | ~6 | `dsv4flash_direct_s1` |
| DSv4flash+min-primer | DSv4-flash | min primer only | ~6 | `dsv4_min_primer_s2` |
| DSv4flash+full-primer | DSv4-flash | full primer | ~6 | `dsv4_full_primer_s2` |
| DSv4flash+plugin+xmllint | DSv4-flash | RAG MCP + xmllint hooks | ~6 | `best_setup_dsv4_s1` (results-only; experiment dir was cleaned) |
| openhands-minimax | minimax-m2.7 | none | ~4 | `oh_test17_s1` |
| openhands+plugin-minimax | minimax-m2.7 | RAG MCP + xmllint hooks | ~4 | `oh_plugin_test17_s1` |

Worker counts marked `~` were not recorded in run metadata; they're
inferred from `compute_seconds / true_wall_seconds` in the table below
(`impl. W` column).

## Results — summary per implementation

| agent | W | ok | ts̄ | ts̃ | compute (s) | wall@W (s) | true wall (s) | impl. W | tok in+out | tok cache-read | cost ($) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| orchestrator-DSv4flash | 2 | 17 | **0.8509** | 0.8519 | 15055 | 7527 | 8069 | 1.87 | 4382075 | 128343424 | – |
| vanilla-DSv4flash | 6 | 17 | 0.6466 | 0.7271 | 6825 | 1137 | 1248 | 5.47 | 6892687 | 72128640 | – |
| DSv4flash+min-primer | 6 | 17 | 0.6660 | 0.6985 | 6215 | 1036 | 1790 | 3.47 | 5084795 | 57154176 | – |
| DSv4flash+full-primer | 6 | 17 | 0.6412 | 0.7642 | 6921 | 1153 | 1864 | 3.71 | 6281558 | 71428992 | – |
| DSv4flash+plugin+xmllint (best-setup) | 6 | 0¹ | 0.6169 | 0.6751 | – | – | – | – | – | – | – |
| openhands-minimax-m2.7 | 4 | 17 | 0.8625 | 0.8894 | 5409 | 1352 | 1625 | 3.33 | 15321289 | 13753586 | 1.4759 |
| openhands+plugin-minimax-m2.7 | 4 | 17 | 0.8201 | 0.8255 | 5554 | 1389 | 1587 | 3.5 | 13828815 | 12570199 | 1.3150 |

¹ The best-setup experiment dir was cleaned up; only the eval JSONs survived. The "ok" column counts task dirs with a populated `status.json`, so it shows 0. TreeSim is still authoritative.

`compute (s)` = Σ per-task `elapsed_seconds`. `wall@W (s)` = compute / declared workers. `true wall (s)` = `max(ended) − min(started)` from task timestamps; for the orchestrator, falls back to filesystem mtimes since the runner doesn't record `started`/`ended`. `impl. W` = compute / true wall (the effective concurrency the campaign actually achieved).

Bold = top score for that task. The orchestrator is top-1 on 6/17 tasks and within 0.05 of top on another 7. OpenHands+minimax (a much larger model) takes the rest.

## Paired delta vs same-model baselines

### orchestrator − vanilla-DSv4flash

mean Δ = **+0.204** | wins/losses/ties (|Δ|>0.01) = **13 / 3 / 1** | median Δ = +0.158

Largest wins: TutorialSneddon +0.754, ExampleDPWellbore +0.684, ExampleMandel +0.608. Three losses are all in coupled-physics scenarios where the orchestrator's per-segment templates fall short: ExampleThermoporoelasticConsolidation (−0.190), buckleyLeverettProblem (−0.102, multiphase flow — flagged in XN-017), ExampleThermalLeakyWell (−0.035).

### orchestrator − DSv4flash+plugin+xmllint (best-setup)

mean Δ = **+0.234** | the best prior plugin configuration on the same backbone. The orchestrator beats the plugin-on-DSv4 setup convincingly — the harness change (segmented authoring + per-subagent primers + per-subagent schema slices) is doing more work than any prior single-agent harness improvement on this model.

### orchestrator − DSv4flash+full-primer

mean Δ = **+0.210** | similar order of magnitude — the gain over a single agent is largely driven by the segmentation + per-segment focus, not just by primer richness. (The full-primer variant has *more* primer content than any individual orchestrator subagent receives, yet scores +0.21 *worse*.)

### orchestrator − openhands-minimax-m2.7

mean Δ = **−0.012** | within seed-level noise. **Significant**: the orchestrator on a smaller cheaper model (DSv4-flash) matches the previous best implementation that used minimax-m2.7, a substantially larger model.

## Efficiency — wall-clock and tokens

### Wall clock

The orchestrator's compute time is roughly 2.2× the single-agent vanilla path (15055s vs 6825s sum) because each task spawns 5 sequential subagent invocations plus orchestrator-side splicing. At W=2, the campaign took 134 min wall vs vanilla's 21 min wall at W=6. **The orchestrator is much slower per-task end-to-end.** This is the chief operational cost.

Effective concurrency was lower than declared: the orchestrator achieved `impl. W ≈ 1.87` vs declared 2 (close to ideal). Vanilla DSv4 hit 5.47 vs declared 6 (consistent with thermal/fs contention). OpenHands campaigns ran at ~3.3 vs declared 4.

If we re-ran the orchestrator at W=4 the campaign wall would scale down to roughly 67 min (wall@W ≈ compute/W), at the cost of more concurrent docker-in-docker subagent spawns. The conservative W=2 choice was deliberate (XN-017 cycle 4, after one OOM-suspected kill at the W=2 smoketest).

### Tokens

| | tokens (input + cached) | per-task | per quality-point |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| orchestrator-DSv4flash | 132.7M | 7.81M | 0.92M |
| vanilla-DSv4flash | 79.0M | 4.65M | 0.72M |
| DSv4flash+full-primer | 77.7M | 4.57M | 0.71M |
| openhands-minimax | 29.1M | 1.71M | 0.20M |

(Orchestrator total = 4.4M paid input + 128M cache-read. Cache-read tokens are charged at ~10–20% of base on most providers, so the headline cost is 2.2× compute multiplied by maybe 1.3–1.5× billed-token cost vs vanilla, depending on cache pricing.)

The orchestrator's high cache-read total (128M vs 72M vanilla) reflects that each subagent pulls a focused primer + schema slice into context, and the same 5 primers get re-read across the 5 subagents per task. The system-prompt cache is doing real work — without it, total token cost would roughly double.

OpenHands uses a fundamentally different ACI (TerminalAction / FileEditorAction etc.) and far less cache. It's the most token-efficient implementation per-task — but it's running a larger model so the per-token cost is higher.

### Per-task wall variance — orchestrator

| task | elapsed (s) |
| --- | --- |
| AdvancedExampleCasedContactThermoElasticWellbore | 1565.7 |
| kgdExperimentValidation | 1269.0 |
| AdvancedExampleDeviatedElasticWellbore | 1257.0 |
| ExampleThermalLeakyWell | 1249.3 |
| buckleyLeverettProblem | 1001.6 |
| TutorialSneddon | 980.4 |
| TutorialPoroelasticity | 981.4 |
| ExampleIsothermalLeakyWell | 948.4 |
| pknViscosityDominated | 932.2 |
| ExampleThermoporoelasticConsolidation | 796.8 |
| ExampleDPWellbore | 659.2 |
| AdvancedExampleModifiedCamClay | 638.4 |
| AdvancedExampleExtendedDruckerPrager | 574.3 |
| ExampleMandel | 579.4 |
| AdvancedExampleViscoDruckerPrager | 551.5 |
| AdvancedExampleDruckerPrager | 542.6 |
| ExampleEDPWellbore | 527.8 |

Range: 528–1566 s (median 932 s). Heavier scenarios (cased wellbore, kgd) take ≈3× longer than the simplest cases (triaxial drivers).

## Discussion

1. **Segmentation is doing the work, not the primer.** The orchestrator
   beats `DSv4flash+full-primer` by +0.21 mean despite each subagent
   seeing *less* primer content than the single-agent full-primer
   baseline. The segment-specific focus + per-segment schema slice is
   what helps — it lets a small/cheap model handle a structured authoring
   task without overflowing on context that's irrelevant to its piece.

2. **Quality matches a substantially larger model.** orchestrator on
   DSv4-flash hits 0.851; OpenHands on minimax-m2.7 (a much larger
   model, used heavily in the project's prior peak runs) hits 0.863.
   Within 1.2pp on a noisy 17-task eval. Picked-up cost: smaller model =
   cheaper per token, even at 2× tokens per task.

3. **Three losses are interpretable.** ExampleThermoporoelasticConsolidation,
   buckleyLeverettProblem, ExampleThermalLeakyWell — coupled
   thermo-poromech-multiphase scenarios. The current orchestrator's
   primers and schema slices are biased toward single-physics + simple
   poroelastic patterns. The drivers/solvers primers do not surface the
   multiphase fluid + thermal coupling boilerplate that the GT expects.
   Those primers can be enriched without changing the architecture.

4. **The token-per-task cost is bounded by the cache.** The orchestrator
   would be ~2× more expensive than vanilla DSv4-flash on a system
   without prompt caching. With Anthropic-style caching (DeepSeek's
   compatibility shim does cache), it's a much smaller cost premium.

5. **Wall-clock is the dominant operational cost.** 134 min @ W=2 vs
   21 min vanilla @ W=6. Easy to halve with W=4 once we're confident the
   docker-in-docker pressure isn't the cause of the cycle-3 OOM-suspected
   kill. A future iteration should re-run at W=4 to lower the wall.

## Limitations

- Single seed for the orchestrator. One seed at n=17 is enough to claim
  +0.20 over vanilla (the gap is large, win/loss is 13/3/1) but
  individual per-task scores carry seed noise. Multi-seed validation is
  the next gate before declaring a tightly-bounded mean.
- Worker counts for the baseline runs were inferred from compute/wall
  ratios, not recorded in metadata. The "wall@W" column should be read
  as an order-of-magnitude estimate, not a precise apples-to-apples.
- DSv4-flash via the Anthropic-compatible endpoint reports
  `output_tokens=0` — the "tok in+out" column is effectively input only
  for the DSv4 variants. OpenHands reports both, so the
  cross-implementation token comparison is not symmetric. We track
  input + cache_read explicitly to keep the comparison meaningful.
- best-setup baseline (DSv4-flash + plugin + xmllint) has only eval
  scores; the experiment dir was cleaned, so we can't compare timing or
  tokens against this prior peak directly. The orchestrator beats it on
  the only axis we have (TreeSim) by +0.234.
- The orchestrator's `--workers 2` choice was deliberate but suboptimal
  for wall-clock. A W=4 re-run is straightforward.
- Multiphase coverage gap (buckleyLeverettProblem, leaky-well thermal):
  the regression is real and traceable to thin primer content in
  drivers/solvers for these physics combinations.

## Next steps

1. Enrich `plugin_orchestrator/primers/{drivers,solvers}.md` with
   multiphase-flow + thermal-coupling boilerplate to address the 3
   losses. Re-run those 3 tasks only.
2. Multi-seed validation: 3 seeds × 17 tasks = 51 runs at the current
   135-min single-seed wall. With W=4 budget that's ~3.4 hours per
   seed × 3 = ~10 hours. Schedulable overnight.
3. Adversarial review of the orchestrator code (mandated by
   `/adversarial-review` gate; deferred from XN-017). Particular focus
   on the `verify_outputs.py` Stop hook and the splicing logic.
4. Try the orchestrator with minimax-m2.7 as the backbone model.
   Question: does the subagent architecture also lift the larger model,
   or is this a small-model-specific gain?

## Response to adversarial review (RN-005)

The adversarial-reviewer subagent ran in a fresh Claude context after this
doc was drafted, with full read access. It produced 3 P1 blockers, 2 P2,
2 P3. Per-finding classification (accept→fix / accept→limitation /
reject-with-reason):

| # | Severity | Finding | Response |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| F1 | **P1** | Cross-test-task GT leakage. `src/runner/contamination.py:get_blocked_files_for_task` only blocks the *current* task's GT files; the other 16 test tasks' GT XMLs remain visible in the filtered tree. Trace evidence: orchestrator on `ExampleIsothermalLeakyWell` copied `thermalLeakyWell_base.xml` (GT for `ExampleThermalLeakyWell`, a sibling test task) into its working directory. Affects both arms but the orchestrator's "ONE search ONE cp" bootstrap step systematizes the shortcut. | **accept → fix.** The blocklist file `misc/memory_artifacts/test_blocklist.json` already has a `union_xml` field listing all 17 test-task GT filenames. Wire it through the runner so every 17-task run blocks the union, not just the current task. Re-run both arms (orch + vanilla) and re-publish the delta. Numbers in §Results above must not be propagated until this fix lands. |
| F2 | **P1** | `--disallowedTools Write` not enforced. `run_orchestrator_eval.py:194-195` passes `Skill`, `AskUserQuestion`, `Write` as repeated `--disallowedTools` flags but Claude Code expects a comma-separated value. Trace: `Write` tool_use fired and produced files in 4 tasks (TutorialSneddon, AdvancedExampleCasedContactThermoElasticWellbore, ExampleThermalLeakyWell, kgdExperimentValidation). The "delegator-only orchestrator" framing in §Discussion is unsupported on those tasks. | **accept → fix.** Switch to `--disallowedTools "Skill,AskUserQuestion,Write"` (comma-joined) and confirm via per-task tool counts that `Write` count is 0 across all subagent and orchestrator turns. Re-run; if some tasks regress when delegation is actually enforced, that's the honest signal. |
| F3 | **P1** | Token double-counting in `analyze_17task.py`. `tally_jsonl_usage` sums every `message.usage` line, but the stream-json transcript re-emits identical message-IDs (subagent fan-out). TutorialSneddon: 229 usage records → 100 distinct message-IDs (2.48× inflation). Vanilla also affected (~3.7×). Headline absolute "4.4M paid input + 128M cache-read" is wrong; cross-implementation comparison vs OpenHands (which reports tokens once) is broken. | **accept → fix.** Dedup by `message.id` before summing, recompute the §Tokens block. Deltas may survive (both DSv4 paths overcount); cross-implementation token comparison vs OpenHands needs to be redone or marked "not directly comparable" until orchestrator/vanilla numbers are dedup'd. |
| F4 | P2 | Bootstrap "ONE search ONE cp" allows copying near-GT siblings. `_XML_VARIANT_SUFFIXES` doesn't cover `_shapes`, `_staticCondensation`, etc. TutorialSneddon copied `Sneddon_embeddedFracShapes_base.xml`, 47-line diff against the GT `Sneddon_embeddedFrac_base.xml`. The +0.204 win conflates bootstrap-shortcut, segmentation, schema slices, and primer enrichment. | **accept → limitation (and partial fix).** Extend `_XML_VARIANT_SUFFIXES` to cover the additional naming patterns the reviewer found. The architectural attribution claim ("segmentation, not primer") in §Discussion §1 is downgraded to a hypothesis pending the post-fix re-run; explicitly note this in any future write-up. |
| F5 | P2 | Primer surface not matched between arms. Vanilla used the baked GEOS Primer (~480 lines AGENTS.md); orchestrator used `--strip-baked-primer` + 647-line external primer + 147-line ORCHESTRATOR_SYSTEM + 5 per-segment subagent primers. The "segmentation, not primer" claim doesn't match primer surface. | **accept → limitation.** A primer-matched control is a separate experiment (e.g., orchestrator with the baked primer, or single-agent-with-orchestrator-aggregate-primer). Add to "Next steps" rather than fix in place. The current setup compares orchestrator-stack vs vanilla-stack as deployed, not orchestrator-architecture in isolation. |
| F6 | P3 | Campaign-wall "true wall = 8069s" is filesystem-mtime-derived because the orchestrator runner doesn't write `started`/`ended` to status.json. | **accept → fix.** One-line addition to the runner: record start/end timestamps in status.json. Also fold this into the next re-run. |
| F7 | P3 | Multi-XML output benefits from scorer's `<Problem>`-merge. TutorialSneddon outputs 7 XMLs; the orchestrator's "single deliverable" rule in ORCHESTRATOR_SYSTEM.md is widely violated. | **accept → limitation.** This is partially a real win (the agent surfaces multiple valid variants) and partially scorer-merge inflation. The right test is to score with `--single-xml` mode (if `judge_geos.py` supports it) or to deduplicate to the canonical output. Add to "Next steps". |

### Implication for the headline

The +0.204 paired delta and the "matches OpenHands+minimax-m2.7"
claim **cannot stand on this run alone**. Three of the largest-win
tasks have at least one P1 issue:

- **TutorialSneddon** (Δ +0.754 in §Paired delta): bootstrap copied
  near-GT sibling `Sneddon_embeddedFracShapes_base.xml`; F2 also fired
  `Write` directly.
- **ExampleIsothermalLeakyWell** (Δ +0.109): cross-task GT leak —
  the orchestrator copied a *test* task's GT into the workspace.
- **ExampleDPWellbore** (Δ +0.684): suspected near-GT bootstrap (RN-005
  flags it as the third largest-win where bootstrap shortcut is plausible;
  exact filename not enumerated).

All three need to re-score after F1+F2 land. Until then, treat XN-018's
quantitative claims as preliminary. The qualitative observation (the
orchestrator works end-to-end on 17/17 tasks, the architecture is
mechanically sound) survives.

### Action plan (logged to status.md → THINK queue)

1. Wire `union_xml` blocklist into the 17-task path.
2. Fix `--disallowedTools` flag syntax (comma-join).
3. Dedup `message.id` in `analyze_17task.py:tally_jsonl_usage`.
4. Re-run both arms (orchestrator + vanilla DSv4-flash) under fixed
   blocklist, fixed Write-deny, multi-seed (≥2 seeds).
5. Update XN-018 with the corrected delta and mark validated only after
   the re-run.
6. Add `_XML_VARIANT_SUFFIXES` enrichment + `started`/`ended` timestamps
   to the runner.

## Reproduction

```bash
# 1. Launch the remaining 12 tasks (5 already in orch_dsv4_5task_s1)
bash scripts/orchestrator/launch_remaining_12.sh orch_dsv4_remain12_s1

# 2. Score
bash scripts/orchestrator/score_run.sh orch_dsv4_remain12_s1

# 3. Cross-implementation analysis (writes the table to <out>)
python3 scripts/orchestrator/analyze_17task.py \
    --orch-runs data/eval/orchestrator_dsv4flash/orch_dsv4_5task_s1 \
                 data/eval/orchestrator_dsv4flash/orch_dsv4_remain12_s1 \
    --orch-results data/eval/results/orch_dsv4_5task_s1/orchestrator_dsv4flash \
                   data/eval/results/orch_dsv4_remain12_s1/orchestrator_dsv4flash \
    --orch-workers 2 \
    --out /tmp/xn018_body.md
```

The auto-generated `/tmp/xn018_body.md` contains the per-task elapsed +
token tables for all implementations; only the headline tables and
discussion are reproduced above. See it for the full per-task wall and
token breakdown.
